speaker: interviewer
text: --the Shoah interview with Henry Frankel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once we were done bathing, we walk out on the other side, and they give us some imitation of towels to wipe ourself. But the day was very warm in May, and you dry very quickly. But instead, you received shirt, pants, and a tunic with the numbers of you. And the two triangles to be sewn on on the side. And once we put this on, we had to go to the next stop, and then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the clog shoes were given to us then. And what they used to call it K-L shoes, which stand for koncentrationslager shoes. And they were made out of wood, and they were very uncomfortable. Many of us suffered greatly because of walking and not being able to get used to it. And-- but the important part was that we survive again another encounter with death by passing the shower rooms, also acting as a-- a-- the gas chambers. Then we got-- they brought us to the dirt field. And I would like to mention that we notice that among the Germans, there was a sudden uncertainty how to conduct certain things. And they didn't even try to find out who was who, or look at anybody. They were just writing it down and extending the number. This was perhaps one of the reasons why I was not identify again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anybody you recognized?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was on an-- an entirely different field. And they brought us to the dirt field. And I got on a Block 20. Block was barracks [? waiting. ?] And the man in charge was Schidwowa, a man that somehow took interest or asking me questions, and where I'm from. He was from-- also from Lodz. He was a former officer of the Polish Army. And when they were building the-- when they were building Majdanek and all the camps, they took the former Jews who were officers of Polish army, and made them the barracks leaders, or kapos, or even higher hierarchy. But he is somehow took interest in my looks, and he start asking me question. And I was very, very friendly with him. He permitted me to conduct certain services like sweeping the barrack. And for this, you were getting additional soup. And this was of great help to anybody. There are many people who envy me. I didn't ask for it. He just came to me. So I don't know why such thing happen, but it did happen. And it help me to somehow to survive. Now, every Sunday, I went-- there was experience of hanging people for people-- everybody had to stay on [GERMAN] place. There was a routine life. You had to work and do certain work outside of the barracks, and you were counted and you were walking out in columns and coming in in columns. If there were greater number of people that walk out and the group leader had to announce how many he has, then the last two people-- had to be always even number. If you walk out with 400, you had to come back with 400. If all a sudden somebody from any other groups got attached just to get back to the camp, and he was 402, those last two people were taken and killed on [INAUDIBLE]. Or they were using another tactics. That they took the numbers, and they called these people on Sunday, and they hang them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was quite one incident good I remember quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a young set of-- they used to call him Benny. And very well dressed with high boots and, you know, breeches, riding. Very nicely made up tunic. And everybody was wondering, what's-- why is Benny so well liked, and what is he doing over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, Benny is one of the guys who were told to hang his father. And I will never forget this, that I couldn't understand how a child could do it. But evidently he done it, because many people were saying, if I ever lived the war-- over the war, I would find him. And let's see what he can do about it. And I don't know if he had any sadistical tendency or not. But he didn't bother anybody or denounce anybody. He were just like a puppet then of a field. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometime in-- at the age of 13, he had cigarettes in his pocket, and he was-- would have lit up one cigarette and then threw it away so somebody could pick it up. And there was a trick for the Germans to see. And they picked him up and beat the blue daylight out of the-- if anybody picked up the pot that Benny threw away. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these procedures existed on the normal condition. In the morning hours, they took us out of barracks at 4 o'clock. Rain, shine, or whatever. At 6 o'clock, we had to be ready to march out to some kind of work. Most of the work was done building roads around the barracks. And there were either rollers engage, or shovel and little wagons to bring earth from place to place. It sometime looked like something was done without any purpose. But it had to be conducted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this existed through May and June. And then in later July, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Schidwowa, the-- the head of the barrack came over to me. He said, tomorrow when they ask for volunteers, you go. So I said, what's going on? He didn't-- couldn't say anything. He said, you just go. So I the following lay-- day in the morning on [GERMAN], when they asked for volunteers, I went. And they took us to a barrack that was quite confusing because we knew that they used to call this barrack [GERMAN] barrack. That means that people were Muselmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: People skinny, unable to walk, sick, and so on were put into this barrack for transportation to crematorium and liquidation. And I couldn't understand why do they want to take us to this barrack. And before you know, the barrack was full of people. And when I look around, they were mostly people fairly healthy and so on. And I couldn't understand this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do they need healthy people in this barrack? Then about an hour later, they chased us out, out of the barrack, and the German SS men-- officers came over. And they were to stand at attention, drop your pants, and drop your-- take off your tunic and your shirt. And he look you over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he told you to get dressed, you got dressed and you stepped aside. And later on, we find out that it was a good omen of being safe. And this was taking part quite a bit of three to four hours by the time the group was assembled. At around 1 o'clock, they distributed some food and some bread to us. And they put us on a-- on a German trucks, brought us to the railroad station, and put us back in the wagons. And this was the first transport that ever left Majdanek for a munition factory of HASAG in Skarzysko-Kamienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that? How did you know that was the first transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know the transports coming out of Skarzysko-- out of Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at the time the-- did you know that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they were telling us, you are the first one to leave Majdanek alive. There was an expression used by many of the barracks' leaders. The only way out of this place is through the chimney. And when we find out that we one of the transport leaving for work place, this was entirely different situation. We knew that something is going on. We didn't know quite well how and what. And finally, we got to Majdanek. To Skarzysko. And it was late at night. And they made another selection for qualified people who know how to handle tools and things like this. And I volunteer again to machine shop. And I had some previous knowledge of dealing with the tools. And at that time, I was taken to a-- a shop that was conducting services in electricity and plumbing and machinery, to the Skarzysko plant. There were three plants in this-- in this vicinity. And the plant that I was on was Skarzysko [GERMAN], C. And with us came many women. And we couldn't understand, this the first time the women were transported from Majdanek together with men. But not in same wagons. Separate, but they came together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the following day we find out that we are in the famous Radom Kielce Skarzysko district of heavy industry of Poland. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody recognized me who was dealing with my father. And he said, you know, you had over here uncle and aunt. I said, what about the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I don't know about the children. But they were trying to escape. And they bribe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which uncle was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lutka. And his name was Gurko, G-U-R-K-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gurko. That was their last name. His name was-- can't momentarily think about. But the-- they were-- she was sister of my father. The youngest sister of my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I ask how did it happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they explain to me they were trying to bribe. Again, Ukrainians were in charge of the security of the camp. And they were patrolling the fences outside in the-- the compound and the camp. The camp was-- they-- factory producing heavy grenades, heavy artillery grenades and mines of-- used by the [INAUDIBLE]-- of [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you know of these substances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, youngsters in the age of-- of 12 to 14 were inserting the igniters and pressing it down. And of course, the kids who were working on night shift, they were tired and exhausted. And when they didn't put this mine in a proper place, they exploded and tore them apart. And they were just swept like a piece of paper. They just throw it away. Women primarily were working on 22 millimeters anti-aircraft artillery. And beside-- behind this was the executioner place where people were from time to time going to selection and brought over there, shot, and buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But you could hear the shots. And they were primarily on Saturday morning. And we-- when we heard the shots, we could come. And we knew how many people departed from this world without any chance of the-- contacting the life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the survivors from this camp-- I have quite few of them. One is a friend of mine, Adam Bonnet, living in Bayside. Another one is Sam Bergman, living in LA. There are three youngsters-- four youngsters. And one is Chaim Yitskowic in Haifa. One is Tsvi Karmi in Tel Aviv. One is Chaim-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Moishe Rosenblum in Kiryat Motzkin. And one is Adam-- Alek Ward, in England. And we somehow prevail the entire time. And when we found each other in Mauthausen again, we remain intact without-- through the liberation date. We were in Skarzysko until the Russians started to push Germans out of their territories and into Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944. April, 1944. And because the-- the supervisors of this plant were, again, Polish people from the outside, and they were bringing information. And meantimes, some-- they were dealing with us on a basis of helping us out if we had anything to trade for it, or to be able to make something for them. And they were telling us the information, how close are the Russians already on Polish territory. And one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Ukrainians gather all the people. There was in late afternoon, in April. Late of April. And told us that very simply we'll be evacuated from Skarzysko to another place. Where, what, and how, nobody knew. But I mentioned that there was a siding-- a railroad siding to the factory, because of the heavy industry and the artillery ammunition. And they all load us up on them, separated women from men again. And we landed in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory, would the women and men work together? Would men and women work together in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And they were permitted even to live together in a barrack. Under special some overseeing condition of the commander of the camp. The camp was strictly Jewish, under Jewish supervision. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody have-- were there any marriages of anybody that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was not officially married. The only marriage was of the commander of the camp and the-- the woman that he-- was the commander's. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember her name. But she is sometime may come to me. And she says, I knew your father. And I was always puzzled. I was looking for the man that deliver the message about my aunt and my uncle who wanted to escape. And evidently, he was a sick elderly man and he-- they-- he was eliminated during selection. From-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Skarzysko, we went to Sulejow to build anti-tank-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call this-- grooves. Not grooves, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pile. Like pylons. And there was just a week that were kept-- they kept us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell the name? How do you spell the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-U-L-E-J-O-W. Sulejow. That was near Piotrkow. And from there, they transported us directly. One group went to Buchenwald. The other group went to [NON-ENGLISH]. Also heavy industry or Polish industry. I landed with the group that went to Buchenwald. And when we arrived in Buchenwald, there were quite famous [GERMAN] which were producing the valves for the V2 engines. And there was totally devastated by the American Air Force during one of the raids. And there was no sign of possibility of-- of rebuilding any such thing again. And I remember that Buchenwald at the time had a quite large force. Perhaps 75,000 people. And the Germans decided to spread them to small factories what needed people. Germany already was experiencing the devastation of Soviet troops pushing them back. And there was no manpower.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us to-- after month or few-- few more weeks, some six weeks in Buchenwald, they send us to a place, munition factory called Schlieben.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work when you were in Buchenwald at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald I did not work at all. There was only once or twice that I was carrying from the quarries stone up the-- to the-- for special purposes. I don't know even if the quarry was of any value as far as the granite is concerned. But they were send-- they sent us to Schlieben. This was near Halle. Spell S-C-H-L-I-E-B-E-N. Schlieben. And I became the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944, in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any news of-- did you hear what was happening with the war at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were getting information even from the Germans. They were saying-- we notice that the young SS men were not surrounding us anymore. But the elderly people of-- in uniform of SS-- performing the functions of SS, too. But they're not as cruel as the youngsters were. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the camp officer was still [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young high-ranking officer. And when he greeted us, he said, the only way you can live to the end of the war is out-producing our anti-tank bazookas. And just happened that one night in January after-- after the new year. In January or beginning of February, British Air-- Air Force was bombarding at night. They were never flying during the day. And they spotted, evidently, some lights. And they started to drop some bombs. And the entire plant went up in the lights. As a matter of fact, this father of the friend of mine who lives in Bayside lost his life in Schlieben. And after a month of collecting all the things from the factory, they send us to another place that they were trying to build up. And this was Flossberg near Leipzig. Now, many a times at night in the early April-- like 1 and 2-- we heard the cannon, artillery cannon. And we think something is going on. And we couldn't get the-- the slightest information. It was tightly secured the camp. And-- but the camp-- the factory was not at-- not in operation yet. They were trying to build it up. But the-- on April 10, 1945, they load us up quickly in the wagon, and transported us to Mauthausen near Linz, where I was liberated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three days, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: The transportation was unbelievable. There was no water and food to be gotten. And we're just hoping that we can get somewhere-- that we can get some water. Water was perhaps more important than food. And we're hoping for the best of the condition to-- to receive. When we got to Gusen, which was the landing place for Mauthausen, we had to climb to Mauthausen and-- to the main camp. And once we got to Mauthausen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a similar situation as in Majdanek. They told us the days where they were gassing people are over. They don't have nothing to gas people. So if they will put you in gas chambers to get showers, you can take it at face value.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever heard of Mauthausen before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know anything about Mauthausen. I never realized that there is another camp in-- in Austria. This was right smack in Alpine Mountains, near Linz. Between Linz and-- and Vienna. And I went to Mauthausen in 1990 for the 45th anniversary of liberation of the camp. And I didn't want to come back and see that 45th anniversary. It was-- again, we encountered something that we couldn't understand. There was about 15,000 Russian POWs. And they were living in separate quarters of the camp. And they were mistreated very badly. The more German suffer from the attacks of the Russians, the-- and they were-- in their life, the more they were mistreated. And there was hardly any food to be gotten until the day of liberation. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simply we were laying and sitting and dream-- and dreaming. Not even the last few days, like May 2, 3, and 4, there were no [GERMAN]. And-- you know, where you were-- where they were counting people on a [GERMAN]. So people didn't move even out of their barracks. But I remember that when the day of liberation-- on the night of the day of liberation, I had to go to the bathroom. And as a rule, we had to knock on a door and inform the Germans standing on a watchtower to permit us to walk to the latrine. And I was trying to attract the attention of a German. There was no response. Then I tried again. Then I finally decided to open the door, take a little pebble, and throw it. Because I notice in a-- a shadow of the-- of the night that the machine gun was just swaying back and forth from there. And I thought maybe he fell asleep. Maybe this is a trick. Well, there was not a trick. There was not a body there. And finally, I managed to get out and use the latrine. And when I came back and I told my friends about it, and they said to me, try to play smart and don't attract any problems at this present moment. At the night of this-- this very night, we heard quite heavy artillery fire in the vicinity of the camp. And we were just praying that the United States forces should liberate us instead of Soviets, despite the fact that it was quite large Soviet [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] POWs in the camp.
